One .zip with everything the world has become: the rendered map (PNG), raw 32-bit heightmap + temperature + rainfall, every layer as an image, the baked LOD atlas, the asset library, and all parameters. Restore it whole with File → Load project .zip. For tiled / 16K output use Tiles & LOD → Export tile grid in Generate → World.

Cartalith Gen1

A single-file world forge — geography, climate, and civilization on one canvas. Choose your base settings, then commit to build the world; from there you shape, finalize and narrate it.
Set up ▸ Forge ▸ Finalize ▸ Atlas

New world — base settings

These are locked in at generation. You can still refine everything afterward in Generate → World.

Peak altitude auto-scales with map width (real relief grows sub-linearly and tops out near Everest ≈ 8 849 m) so the 3D view stays plausible. Edit it to override.

Set the real-world scale of your heightmap. On commit, plate tectonics are inferred from the terrain so climate, biomes, lithology and resources all have a substrate to read.

Selection

Debug overlay
100%
Blends the active debug layer over the base map — drop it to see terrain underneath. In the Settlement view, click a gold dot for a "why here" summary (resource availability + soil/water/carry). Available from World, Civilization and Cartography — the view picker isn't tied to any one sub-tab.
🔒 World finalized.
The simulation is frozen and the map is served from the baked LOD atlas — explore and edit it cartographically (settlements, labels, icons, territory, routes, timeline). To change geography or weather again, use Un-finalize below.

Finalize world

Bakes the whole LOD pyramid down to the chosen depth, then freezes the simulation — Generate → World locks and the project becomes a cartographic LOD viewer/editor (settlements, labels, icons, territory, routes, timeline stay fully editable; un-finalize any time). Per-view bakes, atlas export/import and the chunk overlay live under Tiles & LOD → Atlas below.
Geology

Source & resolution

Map extent
In Whole world mode the map wraps in longitude (X), so a continent can split into slivers on the left/right edges. Center landmasses circularly shifts the world in X to put the emptiest meridian on the seam (latitude/Y is never moved). Re-bake the atlas after centering if you'd baked one.
Working resolution
Generate a world below, or Import → Load heightmap (white = high). Both feed the same field you can warp, erode, sculpt and calibrate. 4K/8K are memory-heavy — for huge final maps use Tiles → Export tile grid to reach 16K without ever holding the whole grid.
—
Experimental. Heavy grid passes run on your GPU when supported and fall back to the CPU automatically (validated against the CPU on startup). Untick to force CPU.

Planet

1.00 g
24 h
23.4°
0.015
1.00×
1.00×
1.00
Tides: the moons' gravitational pull (∝ mass ÷ distance³, ÷ g) sets the open-ocean tidal range; it amplifies in shallow shelf seas and funnels at coasts into a spring tidal-range field — wide intertidal flats and "wandering flood zones" hugging gentle coasts (coastal hazard for settlements/routes; exported as tidal_range.f32). Preview in the Tides debug view.
Surface gravity scales fluvial & glacial erosion (×g), temperature lapse (×g), crater size (×g−0.22), wave energy (×1/g) and rescales peak altitude (~1/g). Talus angle stays fixed: friction-dominated, gravity-independent. Geoid makes sea level a field, not a number: a fast-spinning planet's seas bulge at the equator (J2 ∝ Ω²R/g) on top of lumpy-mass harmonics and mantle noise — coastlines, climate ocean masks and biomes all follow the local sea level. Preview it in the Geoid debug view before enabling. Day length and tilt also drive the weather model (circulation cells, seasons).

Scale & calibration

Vertical
42%
m
Sea level = which height is 0 m (below is sea). Peak = highest point (auto-scales with map width on new worlds — real relief tops out near Everest ≈ 8 849 m; edit to override). Map width is set once, at creation (in the setup gate). The km/mi toggle is display-only.

World Structure

0.30
0.50
0.60
0.60
0.20
When enabled a Continentality field is generated first: continental plates settle in high-field zones, oceanic in low, and a large-scale elevation bias anchors the land/sea split. Continental interiors become drier. Disabled (default): existing random plate generation — identical to current behaviour. All tectonic sliders below remain editable as fine-tuning on top of whichever archetype is active.

Tectonics

14
×1.00
0.45
18px
0.85
0.22
0.60
Physical coupling fields
0.20
0.08
0.50
Flexure adds broad isostatic arches around mountain ranges and subsidence in rift basins — the main driver of continental shelves. Heterogeneity applies low-frequency crustal diversity inside old stable plates (cratons, sedimentary basins). Rock resistance scales erodibility in stream-power erosion: old shields resist incision, young volcanic arcs erode rapidly.
Replaces blurred-stress uplift blobs with type-specific landforms grown along each plate margin: collision fold belts + foreland basins, subduction trenches + volcanic arcs, rift grabens, and transform fault valleys with offset pressure ridges. Preview in the Orog debug view.
1.00
1.00
0.60
Warp bends the straight plate boundaries into organic coastlines and ranges.

Volcanism & impacts

20
0.40
100
0.50
Built into the world after mountain-building. Volcanoes cluster on plate boundaries (arcs & rifts) with power-law sizes; impacts scatter by Poisson with D⁻² sizes. Both degrade with age. Re-erode to weather them.
Hydrology

Erosion

When on, generate() runs a light stream-power pass then stamps parabolic valley cross-sections along the Strahler network, so rivers sit in real carved terrain instead of being painted on a flat surface. Off → identical to v0.144.
Droplet hydraulic
60k
0.35
0.30
8
0.012
Hillslope diffuse
0.15
6
Smooths slopes by linear diffusion (∂z/∂t = D∇²z) — softens cliffs and fills hollows between fluvial passes.
Stream-power carve
0.00
0.012
15
0.30
0.50
Carves dendritic river valleys by fluvial incision (implicit stream-power, Braun & Willett 2013) with multiple-flow-direction drainage. Uplift is off by default — raise it only to grow mountains that compete with incision; at 0 it purely carves existing relief. Channeling sets incision aggressiveness; Deposition refills valleys/builds deltas but never raises a channel above the land; Rain → erosion makes wet regions carve faster. See the Flow view.
Velocity (momentum)
60
0.50
0.60
Pillar 2 — full velocity-field hydraulic erosion (virtual-pipe shallow water, Mei et al. 2007 / LanLou123): water carries momentum (semi-Lagrangian advection), so it overshoots bends and presses the outer bank (erosion) while dropping sediment on the inner bank — meanders & oxbows. Closed basins pool into lakes until they spill the lowest sill. Gravity (Planet → g) scales the flux. See the Velocity debug view. A new op — it doesn't touch the droplet/stream/glacial passes.
Evolve & sediment
5
Evolve couples climate and erosion: each cycle carves, rebounds isostatically, then recomputes the weather on the freshly carved terrain — so rain shadows and rivers migrate as mountains grow/wear down. Tidal flats accretes mudflats in the intertidal zone (needs Tides on). Dynamic lithology hardens deeply-exhumed rock so the next pass bites less there.

Glacial erosion

0.65
0.15
0.60
8
Carves high, cold terrain (above the snowline) into U-shaped valleys. Ice follows the drainage network and widens valleys laterally instead of just cutting down. Glacier heads scoop into cirque bowls. Lower the snowline to glaciate more of the landscape; raise it to restrict ice to the peaks. Run after erosion for best results — erode first, then glaciate. Carve fjords overdeepens valleys below sea level only on cold, steep, hard-rock (granite/gneiss/basalt) coasts — drowned glacial troughs (Norway/Patagonia-style), never on tropical or weak-rock shores. See the Fjord debug view for the probability mask.

Coastal

0.50
0.08
0.03
4
Three coastal processes in one pass. Sea cliffs: wave energy erodes exposed coastal cells and leaves steep cliff faces (run multiple times to carve retreating cliffs). Estuaries: high-flow river mouths near sea level are widened with an exponential taper — funnel-shaped inlets emerge where major rivers meet the coast. Tidal marsh: the narrow, flat intertidal band is roughened with organic micro-relief. Run after stream-power carve so river mouths are established.
Climate

Climate & biomes

Latitude band this map spans (Region mode — see Map extent above)
55°
5°
Temperature
30°C
−25°C
6.5°C/km
off
Temperature falls with latitude (insolation) and altitude (lapse rate). In Whole world mode the map spans pole-to-pole; in Region mode you set the latitude band. Seasons runs summer/winter passes (thermal equator shifts by the planet's axial tilt) and classifies every land cell into a Köppen–Geiger climate (see the Köppen debug view). Ice albedo adds the cryosphere feedback — ice/snow reflects sunlight and cools further, so polar caps and high massifs grow colder and broaden (0 = off).

Weather · rainfall sim

70
×1.00
0.12
0.35
×1.00
0.60
0.50
0°
Moisture evaporates off the seas (more from warm water), rides the wind, and rains where air climbs terrain or saturates — windward coasts wet, lee interiors dry. Planetary wind = latitude belts (trades / westerlies / polar easterlies; belt count follows day length) bent by thermal lows and highs (pressure influence, Coriolis-deflected) — monsoon-style sea→land flow emerges where summer land runs hot. Manual = one fixed direction (Wind →, region only). Runs on click; more iterations push moisture deeper inland.
Ecology
1.00×
all
River density scales the channel-initiation drainage area — higher = fewer, larger channels; lower = denser network. Min stream order hides small tributaries (order 1 = all). Sharper biomes adds a finer ecotone-jitter octave for resolution-revealing biome boundaries. Lakes appear as inland water bodies when water-body detection is enabled.
Manual Terrain
Off by default so the map can't be accidentally reshaped while browsing. Turn this on to arm the Feature brush and Direct paint tools below.

Feature brush (plotline)

1. Pick feature   2. Draw a guide stroke on the map   3. Apply
28
0.45
Tap Draw guide, then drag a freehand stroke on the terrain — the shaded tube shows the affected band. Apply synthesizes the feature along your line with fractal detail. Rivers carve downstream from the start of the stroke toward the end.

Direct paint

Drag on the terrain to sculpt in real time
22
35%
Drag on the canvas to sculpt — requires Enable manual terrain editing above to be checked. Pan with middle-drag, space-drag or mouse wheel. Disabled in debug views. Cliff / Ridge / Canyon follow drag direction. Mesa / Volc. stamp once on tap. Water deposits a lake filled to the clicked height. With Edit LOD tiles on, the same brush sculpts the refined Tiled-LOD detail; edits persist per tile, Ctrl-Z undoes.
3D view
Press the 3D pill above the map to drape the fully-painted map over a relief mesh. These dials style the 3D view only — they never change the heightmap data.
0.50
315°
Relief scales how steep the terrain looks. Detail raises mesh smoothness (heavier). Flatten oceans keeps sea from rippling under exaggeration.
Tiles & LOD
Live preview
1.00×
Auto-detail on zoom drops you into the tiled LOD view as you zoom in, so finer relief surfaces the closer you look. Zoom detail sets how pronounced the on-zoom procedural relief is (1× = legacy). Refine detail still force-amplifies the current view; never an 8K canvas.
Export tile grid
~4 tiles · — MB
Drag a rectangle on the map, then Refine & export: each tile is amplified from the coarse world in world-space (seam-free), packed as 16-bit height + a rendered PNG, with a schema-2 manifest. The grid (X×Y) and per-tile size are independent — tile pixels follow the selection's true shape, so non-square regions are never squished. The estimate above updates live.
Atlas (IndexedDB bake)
Atlas: —
Bake writes the refined tiles in view to a permanent IndexedDB atlas; baked chunks are served from the atlas instead of being regenerated (the chunk-debug overlay shows them green), and no detail refines beneath them. Clear atlas drops this world's bakes back to procedural. The atlas travels automatically with File → Export .zip/Load project .zip — no separate export needed. Browser-only — needs IndexedDB. To bake the whole pyramid and freeze the simulation, use Bake ALL levels & finalize world at the top of Generate → World.
Chunk debug overlay
Tools
Pick a tool, then click the map. Inspect selects an existing place — its editor opens in Selection above; Settlement/POI drop new ones; Territory paints faction land (pick a faction in Peoples — ∅ Unclaimed erases); Way clicks waypoints, then tap ✓ Commit way in Infrastructure (Esc also works).
Peoples

Factions

Double-click a faction to rename it. Add faction appends a new polity with a distinct colour; Remove last drops the most recently added one (settlements/territory using it revert to Unclaimed).
5
Settlements
Icons & lines
1.00×
1.00×
Settlement pins, POI markers and their labels stay a roughly constant on-screen size at any zoom level — this slider is a further multiplier on top of that. Way line width scales the road/track/sea-lane strokes drawn by the Civilization layer (Infrastructure below), independently of the reference roads in Generate → World.
Auto-populate counts (blank = automatic)
Auto-populate world places settlements based on terrain suitability and carrying capacity. Fill in any of the count fields to fix how many of each tier are seeded (e.g. Capitals 3, Villages 10, Hamlets 20) — tiers are then assigned best-site-first and the centrality promotion step is skipped so your counts hold; crossroads trade settlements may still add a few. Use Drop place to manually place settlements or POIs; Inspect selects and edits existing places.
Sourced correction to carrying capacity for effects soil/temp/water miss — the rainforest paradox (wet, productive biomes suppressed by pathogen load), arid/cold survival friction, and (v0.77) a wetland/marsh story: productive fish/rice/waterfowl land with high water-managed intensification potential but malaria/flood friction. Off by default; on re-derives carrying capacity, settlement suitability and the Pop density layer. See docs/research/settlement-density.md.
Adds a rare tier above Capital for empire-scale worlds (settlement-density §5). On the next Auto-populate, a dominant capital — very high trade-through (betweenness) centrality and a large polity — is promoted to a Metropolis (Nineveh/Baghdad register, ~3× a capital's base population). Off by default ⇒ auto-populate is byte-identical; capped rare (≤1 per faction). See docs/research/settlement-density.md.
On the next Auto-populate (with the count fields blank), settlements seed at the ~10 km site-catchment village spacing (Vita-Finzi & Higgs 1970) instead of the default ~market-town spacing — a ~3–4× denser hamlet/village grid, capped at 200 pins so the editor list stays usable. Off by default ⇒ auto-populate byte-identical. For genuine village density without a pin per hamlet, use the population estimate below. See docs/research/settlement-density.md §6.
Models a world rebuilding after a demographic collapse (settlement-emergence §5–6). On the next Auto-populate, populations run below the ecological ceiling by the phase fraction, and where a nucleus is scaled below the labour its tier needs it collapses into its ruins — a former city surviving only as a fortified village. Survival/Subsistence phases abandon tiny inland nodes; survivors cluster on water and defensible ruins. Stable ⇒ auto-populate byte-identical (mature society at the ceiling).
Modeled population (auto · scales with map size)
Computed automatically on Auto-populate. Integrates the modeled persons/km² field (the Pop density layer) over all land — the population the map's habitable land sustains at pre-industrial density, and the base each settlement's own population is drawn from (via its catchment carrying capacity). Reflects the Biome carrying-capacity toggle; per-faction totals sum over painted territory.
All settlements (0)
Click a row (or ✎) to open its editor in the pinned Selection panel above.
All POIs (0)
Points of interest — click a row (or ✎) to open its editor in the pinned Selection panel, exactly like the settlement list.
Polity
Territory
Paint territory: select a faction from Peoples (pick Unclaimed to erase) then drag on the map to assign land. Auto-polity generates faction boundaries automatically from settlement locations and terrain. Political eras live in the timeline below.
Politics timeline
Each year stores a snapshot of the territory paint plus which settlements/roads existed then. Negative years are BC. Navigate years via the slider or the pills to edit independent political eras; the full filter/playback controls live in the Explore tab.
Simulate collapse / recovery
50%
1.0%/yr
Runs a year-by-year simulation from the CURRENT settlements and writes one timeline entry per step, so you can scrub the collapse (or recovery) exactly like manually-authored history. Collapse: each settlement's stress (trade-network loss, density/connectivity exposure, or undefended exposure — set by Character) drives excess mortality and out-migration each step; survivors redistribute to reachable settlements by a gravity model (closer + more headroom + fortified = more attractive); a nucleus that shrinks below its tier's floor demotes (a city can collapse into a fortified village in its own ruins) or is abandoned outright. Recovery: logistic regrowth toward each settlement's full catchment capacity. See docs/research/collapse-timeline-dynamics.md.
Infrastructure
Manual way drawing
Select a way type then click the map to add waypoints, then tap ✓ Commit way (desktop: Esc also works) to finish — each segment routes through the terrain via Dijkstra least-cost pathfinding. Roads prefer valleys and passes; sea lanes cross open water.
Auto-generate
Auto-routes draws land & sea ways between existing settlements.
No ways yet. Use Auto-routes, or draw one manually with the Draw way tool.
Tools
Inspect selects an existing label or icon on the map — its editor opens in Selection above; Label names a region (click an empty spot); Icon reveals the gallery below to pick a variant first.
Region names
Pick the Label tool above, then click an empty spot to name a mountain range, sea, forest or any area. Click an existing label's box (or drag it, or its corner handle to resize) to select it; a ✓/✗ appear above the box to confirm or cancel edits. Saved with the project.
All labels (0)
Click a row (or ✎) to open its editor in the pinned Selection panel above.
Manual icons
Click a tile to arm it, then click empty ground to place it; click an existing icon to select it (drag to move, drag its corner handle to resize, Delete to remove). Custom icons come from a loaded pack's custom sets — Import → Import asset pack (header).
Pick the Icon tool above to reveal the icon gallery and place one.
Placed icons (0)
Paint brush
6
0.70
Paints the Biome/Terrain flat colour as a tint over the shaded procedural map, or (Splat) forces one pack ground texture at full coverage. Never touches ocean or lake cells. Texture strength also controls how strongly the Splat layer and the ordinary procedural ground textures show through. Import → Import asset pack (header) loads ground textures; empty texture slots stay procedural even where painted.
No pack loaded — mountains, trees and ground use built-in procedural art. See docs/ASSET_PACK_FORMAT.md.

Rendering — advanced
Rendering
off

off
off
off
off
off
off
off
annual
Sky view factor darkens enclosed valleys/gorges that see less sky; cast shadows throw long soft shadows off major ranges toward the sun azimuth; curvature shading brightens convex ridges and darkens concave hollows; geology materials recolours exposed rock by the lithology underneath (granite speckle, basalt fields, sandstone strata, karst limestone, gneiss banding + dune ripples) so mountains read geological; wetness darkens persistently saturated ground (high TWI); season blends the rendered climate toward January ⇠ annual ⇢ July when Seasons (Weather) is on — snowlines migrate, vegetation dries/greens. All render-only, default off.
auto
off
off
off
The SDF (signed-distance) layers treat coast, rivers and biome edges as continuous geometry: coastlines add resolution-independent beach → coastal-plain bands; river bands draw bank/wetland/floodplain margins from the drainage SDF; biome blend widens ecotones near biome boundaries. Render-only (screen overlays, like the river/minor-channel layers); all default off.
Painter styles (NPR)
Painter (NPR)
off
off
off
off
off
off
off
off
off
off
Hand-drawn "Painter" styles, each its own intensity: contour veins = constant-width elevation isolines; ink = pen outlines on landform edges; hachure = downslope hatching denser on steep ground; watercolor = pigment pooling + paper granulation; cel/toon = posterized flat colour bands; engraving = antique cross-hatch; stipple = pen dot-density shading; sepia = antique warm brown toning; risograph = indigo→amber duotone screen-print with halftone dots; pointillism = Seurat coloured-dot field. Land-only, seamless across tiles/bakes; all default off — stack them freely.
Overlays
1.00×
Parchment multiplies a paper-fibre grain and warm tint into the map colours. The icon layer finds ridgelines and forest cover and draws mountain, hill and tree glyphs over the relief — largest peaks claim the range spine first. Wave lines add concentric foam contours hugging the shore. All are render-only (PNG bakes/layer exports are unchanged) and work with zero assets.
Tools
Draw a planned Route (journey) with the Route tool — click waypoints on the map, then tap ✓ Commit route (desktop: Esc also works) to finish. Info reads out settlement & ecology details for any clicked cell. The Journey planner below sets transport mode, terrain and group conditions.

🔍 Info

Click the map to inspect a location.

Journeys

No journeys yet. Draw one with the Route tool and tap ✓ Commit route.

Journey planner

The route is sampled from the generated world — terrain, biome, elevation, rivers, roads and settlements along the line drive the calculation (V1.915 travel framework).
Select a journey above to plan it.

Asset pack

—
Assets live in the project: Export .zip (header File ▾) embeds them and Load project .zip restores them — no rebuilding from pack ZIPs.

Build & export

Export writes pack.json (schema 2) + PNGs as a STORED zip — byte-compatible with the engine importer and Cartalith packs.

Credits & academic principles

Cartalith Gen1 is a single self-contained HTML file with zero external dependencies — every solver, renderer and UI control is original code. The algorithms below were studied, not copied: each is an independent implementation of a published method. Full inline attribution lives in the source and in docs/research/.

Programming & code sources studied

  • Hydraulic erosion — virtual-pipes shallow-water & droplet methods, informed by LanLou123/Webgl-Erosion, SebLague/Hydraulic-Erosion, weigert/SimpleHydrology, and Beyer (2015).
  • River geometry / drainage synthesis — Pasternack Lab / RiverBuilder (UC Davis), Genevaux et al. (2013), Galin et al. (2019).
  • Optical water shading — Beer–Lambert depth + flow-map UV, after Premože & Ashikhmin, "Rendering Natural Waters" (Stanford).
  • Procedural noise — fractal Brownian motion / gradient (Perlin-style) noise, original implementation.
  • Cartalith V1.915 editor — the routes / settlements / territory / journey-planner layer is ported from the author's own earlier cartographic editor (this repo's Cartalith_V1.915.html).
  • Runtime — vanilla JS/Canvas/WebGL/Web Workers/IndexedDB; no frameworks, no CDN, opens over file://.

Academic principles — terrain, tectonics & climate

  • Plate tectonics — plate partition, boundary classification (collision / subduction / island-arc / rift / transform) and stress fields drive orogeny and crustal age.
  • Lithospheric flexure & isostasy — broad flexural response to boundary loads (arches, foreland basins, continental shelves) and erosional-unloading isostatic rebound.
  • Stream-power incision — implicit fluvial incision, Braun & Willett (2013), with multiple-flow-direction drainage.
  • Drainage networks & hydraulic geometry — Strahler (1957) stream ordering; downstream hydraulic geometry, Leopold & Maddock (1953); Rosgen-inspired channel classes.
  • Velocity-field hydraulic erosion — virtual-pipes shallow water with semi-Lagrangian momentum advection, Mei et al. (2007) — meanders, oxbows, spill-point lakes.
  • Climate — latitude/insolation + altitude lapse-rate temperature, orographic moisture advection, and Köppen–Geiger climate classification with axial-tilt seasons; NPP via the Miami model.

Academic principles — civilization & population

  • Carrying capacity & population density — NPP-anchored forager floor (Tallavaara et al. 2018; Zhu et al. 2021) and a water-gated agrarian ceiling.
  • Central-place theory — settlement spacing by threshold & range (Christaller 1933; Lösch 1940).
  • Network centrality & robustness — Brandes (2001) betweenness; scale-free networks are robust to random loss but fragile to hub removal (Albert, Jeong & Barabási 2000).
  • Gravity model of migration — flow ∝ attractiveness / distanceβ (Zipf 1946; Ravenstein 1885).
  • Logistic population growth — Verhulst (1838) regrowth toward a catchment ceiling.
  • Collapse dynamics — mortality calibrated to the Black Death (Benedictow 2004); collapse-character ordering after Cline (2014) and Wickham (2005).

Detailed, per-method citations and derivations: docs/research/ (river-overhaul, settlement-density, settlement-emergence, collapse-timeline-dynamics) and the inline @architecture / @physics / @credits blocks in the source. Algorithms studied, not copied — original implementations throughout.
